request.question: Before we vote on Thara Pay: what does the latest macro data say about Saudi consumer spending and digital payments (GDP, inflation, household spending, the policy rate), and does anything in it challenge the growth assumptions in the analysis?

events.text:
## (1) Recommendation

The latest macro data is,
on balance, supportive of Thara Pay's GMV-linked revenue thesis, but two data points warrant explicit IC scrutiny before the vote: (
a) the near-term deceleration signal in the seasonally adjusted GDP series, and (b) the staleness of the digital-economy stat
istics underpinning the structural growth case. Neither invalidates the analyst's base case, but both b

ear on the durability of the volume growth assumption that drives the 18.5% implied IRR.

## (2)

Rationale

**Consumer spending backdrop (supportive, with a caveat).**
Average household monthly disposable income was SAR 11,839 and average monthly consumption expenditure was SAR 10,884, with food &
beverages the single largest category at 27.9% of household final monetary consumption expenditure.

events.quote:
Average household monthly disposable income was SAR 11,839; average
household monthly consumption expenditure was SAR 10,884. Of household final monetary consumption expenditure, food & beverages was

events.text: This is directly relevant because Thara
Pay's merchant base sits in e-commerce and marketplace verticals, and
revenue is trans
action-fee based, giving the business a revenue base that scales with underlying merchant GMV.

events.quote:
Revenue is transaction-fee based, with a smaller subscription component for reconciliation and
payout tooling — giving the business a revenue base that scales with underlying merchant GMV rather than

events.text: A healthy consumption

base supports that GMV pool.

**Inflation (supportive).**
The CPI recorded an annual increase of 1.8% in May 2026 versus May 2025, driven mainly by hous
ing, water, electricity, gas & other fuels (+3.7%), transport (+1.5%) and restaurants & accommodation services (+1

events.quote:
Inflation — Consumer & Wholesale Prices
Consumer Price Index – May 2026
GASTAT · 14 June 2026 · stats.gov.sa/documents/d/guest/cpi-may-2026-en-pdf
The CPI recorded an annual increase of 1.8% in May

events.text: Contained inflation preserves real household purchasing power, which is favor

able for nominal GMV growth without demand destruction.

**Monetary policy (supportive).**
S
AMA cut the Repo rate by 25bps to 4.25% and the Reverse Repo rate by 25bps to 3.75%, mirroring a same-week US Federal Re
serve cut under the SAR/USD peg.

events.quote:
Monetary Policy — SAMA
SAMA's Decision on Repo and Reverse Repo Rates
Saudi Central Bank (SAMA) · 10 December 2025 · sama.gov.sa/en-us/mediacenter/news/pages/news-1124.aspx
SAMA cut the Repurchase

events.text: An easing bias plus li
quidity expansion,
M3 broad money supply reaching SAR 3.307 trillion as of end-
March 2026, up 8.25% YoY,

events.quote:
METRIC VALUE EFFECTIVE
Repo rate 4.25% from 10 Dec 2025
Reverse repo rate 3.75% from 10 Dec 2025
Repo rate, change −25bps from 4.50%
Reverse repo rate, change −25bps from 4.00%
Saudi Arabia's Mon

events.text: is broadly constructive for consumer credit and e-commerce sp

end.

**GDP growth (supportive headline, one caution).**
Real GDP grew 3.0% year-on-
year in Q1 2026, driven by broadly even growth across oil and non-oil activities.

events.quote:
Real GDP Growth
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) Publication for Q1 of 2026
GASTAT · 9 June 2026 · stats.gov.sa/en/w/news/199
Real GDP grew 3.0% year-on-year in Q1 2026, driven by broadly even growth a

events.text: Notably for a
payments-infrastructure name,
the highest-growth sector was financial & insurance activities and business services (+5.4%).

events.quote:
The highest-growth sector
was financial & insurance activities and business services (+5.4%), followed by manufacturing excluding oil refining (+4.0%).

events.text: The caution:
on a seasonally adjusted basis, real GDP declined 1.2% versus Q4

events.quote: On a seasonally adjusted basis, real GDP declined 1.2% versus Q4 2025.

events.text: Judgment: this is a single-quarter sequential reading, not a trend, but

it is a near-term deceleration signal against a thesis that depends on continued volume expansion.

**Digital
-economy / payments structural case (supportive but stale).**
The digital economy's share of GDP reached
15.6% in 2023, up 1.6 percentage points from 2022,

events.quote:
Digital Economy, ICT & Household Spending
Digital Economy accounts for 15.6% of Saudi Arabia's GDP — 2023 Digital Economy Statistics Publication
GASTAT · 25 May 2025 · stats.gov.sa/en/w/news/46
The

events.text: and
the ICT sector recorded SAR 236.4b
n in operating revenues for 2023.

events.quote:
The ICT sector recorded SAR 236.4bn in operating revenues for 2023, with ICT
imports up 19.9% YoY and ICT exports/re-exports up 76.1% YoY.

events.text: This corroborates the secular digitization t
ailwind. Judgment: the key limitation is vintage, the digital-economy figures are 2023, roughly three years st

ale relative to a July 2026 IC vote, so they establish direction but not current run-rate.

##

(3) Does the macro challenge the analysis's growth assumptions?

The analyst's model rests on volume, not just merchant count:
the model shows take-rate and processed-volume growth outpacing merchant-count growth, consistent with existing merchants processing a r
ising share of their GMV through the platform.

events.quote:
Financial analysis
The analyst's model shows take-rate and processed-volume growth outpacing merchant-count growth, consistent with
existing merchants processing a rising share of their GMV through

events.text: The macro data does not contradict this, but two items int
ersect with the identified risk that
the business carries a dependency on continued
e-commerce GMV growth in the merchant base:

events.quote:
QUANTIFIED RISK ASSESSMENT & SHARIAH SCREEN
Risk score: 5 of 10
The mid-range risk score of 5/10 reflects a business with a proven, non-speculative revenue model (transaction fees on real
merchant

events.text: - **Non-oil deceleration.** Full-year
2025 non-oil growth was

events.quote:
oil refining) growth +4.0% Q1 2026 YoY
Real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) Statistics for 2025 and Q4
GASTAT · 9 March 2026 · stats.gov.sa/en/w/news/171
Full-year 2025 real GDP grew 4.5% versus 2024,

events.text: whereas
non-oil activities growth was +2.9% YoY in Q1 2026.

events.quote:
METRIC VALUE PERIOD
Real GDP growth (YoY) +3.0% Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025
Oil activities growth (YoY) +2.9% Q1 2026
Non-oil activities growth (YoY) +2.9% Q1 2026
Government activities growth (YoY) +1.5%

events.text: Judgment: this step-down,
plus the −1.2% sequential print, is the single macro observation most relevant to whether the base

-case GMV growth trajectory is conservative or optimistic.
- **Regulatory back
drop.** The macro pack does not cover SAMA payments licensing directly, so it neither supports nor challenges the
regulatory/licensing dependency typical of payments businesses operating under SAMA oversight.

events.quote:
Key risk drivers weighted into the score: (i) new-entrant competition compressing take
rates, (ii) concentration among a relatively small number of larger merchants, and (iii) regulatory/licensing de

events.text: That is

n't in my connected sources. In the MVP this routes to web, Bloomberg, or PitchBook connectors.

**What would change this
read:** the ranking of macro as "supportive" fli
ps toward "cautionary" if the Q1 2026 sequential contraction extends into a second quarter of nega
tive seasonally adjusted GDP, or if the analyst's base-case GMV growth rate was calibrated to the FY2
025 +4.9% non-oil pace rather than the more recent +2.9% Q1 pace. I would ask the analyst to discl
ose which growth vintage feeds the model and to run the bear case against the Q1 2026 ru
n-rate. For reference, the buffer is thin: im
plied IRR
of 18.5% clears the 15% benchmark with 3.5 points of margin, and the scenario-weighted expected return is 16.2%,

events.quote:
Metric Value
Implied IRR at entry 18.5%
Mandate hurdle rate 15.0%
Scenario-weighted expected return 16.2%
Quantified risk score (1 = lowest, 10 = highest) 5 / 10
Mandate fit Pass
Shariah screen

events.text: so

a material downward revision to volume growth could compress the weighted return toward the benchmark.

I c
an only cite Thara Pay's sources here; there is no competing company file connected to this conversation fo

r comparison.

Advisory only: the investment decision rests with the committee.
